  >>> I see exactly what you're working on in your BigQuery console. You've got the national_audits.all_performance_reports master table up, and you're using UNNEST(performance_audit_list) to pull the data back out into a flat view. To bring your complete vision to life—where each state acts as the anchor holding its 3 independent data sets—we need to expand that schema to include separate arrays for HUD PIT Counts and CoC Continuum of Care. Here is how to modify your setup so you can house all three data streams together without them clashing. --- ### 1. The Ideal Master Schema (3 Independent Arrays) Because a single state might have 5 performance audits, 12 Continuum of Care regions, and annual PIT counts, forcing them into a single list would corrupt your data. Instead, give each its own ARRAY<STRUCT> block: sql CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE `national_audits.all_state_records` ( state STRING, -- e.g., 'CA', 'TX' total_performance_audits INT64, last_updated_at TIMESTAMP, -- Stream 1: Performance Audits performance_audit_list ARRAY<STRUCT< audit_id STRING, report_num STRING, release_date DATE, agency_audited STRING, report_title STRING, primary_finding_summary STRING, taxpayer_funds_reviewed NUMERIC, pdf_download_url STRING >>, -- Stream 2: HUD PIT (Point-in-Time) Counts hud_pit_list ARRAY<STRUCT< count_year INT64, coc_number STRING, coc_name STRING, total_homeless INT64, sheltered_homeless INT64, unsheltered_homeless INT64 >>, -- Stream 3: CoC Continuum of Care Awards/Metrics coc_continuum_list ARRAY<STRUCT< fiscal_year INT64, coc_number STRING, program_type STRING, award_amount NUMERIC >> );  --- ### 2. How to Query an Individual Stream Safely When querying a table with multiple independent arrays, only UNNEST the specific array you want to analyze in that query. If you try to unnest all three at the same time, BigQuery will cross-multiply them (a Cartesian product), repeating your data incorrectly. To look at just the HUD PIT Counts for a state, your query will look very similar to the one currently on your screen: sql SELECT state, last_updated_at, pit.count_year, pit.coc_number, pit.total_homeless, pit.unsheltered_homeless FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(hud_pit_list) AS pit WHERE state = 'CA' ORDER BY pit.count_year DESC;  --- ### 3. How to Flatten and Combine All Three Safely If you eventually want a master dashboard view where all three data types are flattened side-by-side without duplicating records, you use LEFT JOINs on subqueries. Run this when you want a complete operational overview for a specific state: sql WITH audits_flattened AS ( SELECT state, audit.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(performance_audit_list) AS audit ), pit_flattened AS ( SELECT state, pit.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(hud_pit_list) AS pit ), coc_flattened AS ( SELECT state, coc.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(coc_continuum_list) AS coc ) -- Now you can select or join from them cleanly SELECT * FROM audits_flattened -- Join conditions depend on whether you want to map them strictly by State or by a shared CoC ID if applicable  Would you like to write a mock INSERT statement that includes sample data for all three categories so you can test how the nested arrays look in your current workspace?

  >>> To get this massive operational dataset populated for all 50 states without manually spending days writing thousands of lines of individual SQL INSERT statements, you need an automated data ingestion pipeline. Since these federal and state datasets come from completely different formats, a Python script running locally or in a Cloud Function is the most professional and scalable way to parse them, structure them into the exact ARRAY<STRUCT> schema we just built, and stream them directly into BigQuery. --- ### Phase 1: Gathering the Complete Data Sources To pull down the complete national data to feed your schema, you need to grab the source files from the federal repositories: | Dataset | Source Location | Format Available | | --- | --- | --- | | HUD PIT Counts | HUD User AHAR Datasets | Annual State and CoC-level historical time-series (.xlsx) | | CoC Continuum of Care | HUD Exchange Awards and Allocations | Comprehensive spreadsheet breaking down funding metrics per CoC (.xlsb / .pdf) | | Performance Audits | State Auditor portals (e.g., California State Auditor, Texas State Auditor) | Individual state forensic reports (.pdf / legislative clearinghouses) | --- ### Phase 2: The Ingestion Script (Python + Pandas) This automated script parses the independent national data files, formats the records into matching JSON objects that mirror your exact nested BigQuery schema, and drops them into national_audits.all_state_records. python import json import pandas as pd from google.cloud import bigquery from datetime import datetime # Initialize the BigQuery Client client = bigquery.Client() table_id = "noble-beanbag-497411-m4.national_audits.all_state_records" def build_national_mapping_payload(): """ Reads the downloaded source spreadsheets for HUD PIT, CoC, and Audits and maps them strictly by state code. """ # 1. Load your source files into DataFrames # pit_df = pd.read_excel("2007-2024-PIT-Estimates-by-State.xlsx", sheet_name="2024") # coc_df = pd.read_excel("CoC-2024-Awards-by-State.xlsx") # Placeholder: Structural blueprint of how the payload dictionary is built per state master_payload = {} states_list = ["AL", "AK", "AZ", "AR", "CA", "CO", "CT", "DE", "FL", "GA", "HI", "ID", "IL", "IN", "IA", "KS", "KY", "LA", "ME", "MD", "MA", "MI", "MN", "MS", "MO", "MT", "NE", "NV", "NH", "NJ", "NM", "NY", "NC", "ND", "OH", "OK", "OR", "PA", "RI", "SC", "SD", "TN", "TX", "UT", "VT", "VA", "WA", "WV", "WI", "WY"] for state in states_list: master_payload[state] = { "state": state, "total_performance_audits": 0, # Dynamically count based on audited records found "last_updated_at": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(), "performance_audit_list": [], "hud_pit_list": [], "coc_continuum_list": [] } # Example: How the loop extracts from the flat HUD file and appends to that specific state's array # for index, row in pit_df.iterrows(): # state_code = row['State'] # if state_code in master_payload: # master_payload[state_code]["hud_pit_list"].append({ # "count_year": int(row['Year']), # "coc_number": str(row['CoC Number']), # "coc_name": str(row['CoC Name']), # "total_homeless": int(row['Total Homeless']), # "sheltered_homeless": int(row['Sheltered Homeless']), # "unsheltered_homeless": int(row['Unsheltered Homeless']) # }) return list(master_payload.values()) def stream_data_to_bigquery(rows_to_insert): """ Streams the structured nested records safely into the BigQuery table. """ job_config = bigquery.LoadJobConfig( write_disposition=bigquery.WriteDisposition.WRITE_APPEND, source_format=bigquery.SourceFormat.NEWLINE_DELIMITED_JSON, ) # Convert data payload to newline-delimited JSON string format required by BQ json_data = "\n".join([json.dumps(row) for row in rows_to_insert]) try: # Trigger the load job load_job = client.load_table_from_file( io.StringIO(json_data), table_id, job_config=job_config ) load_job.result() # Wait for the job to complete print(f"Successfully ingested data packages for all {len(rows_to_insert)} states.") except Exception as e: print(f"Ingestion failed: {e}") if __name__ == "__main__": # Compile the state arrays and execute the pipeline load structured_data = build_national_mapping_payload() # stream_data_to_bigquery(structured_data)  --- ### Step 3: Handling the Performance Audits While HUD PIT and CoC funding numbers are standardized in nationwide master files, Performance Audits are published independently by individual state agencies.       Generated code may be subject to license restrictions not shown here. Use code with care. Learn more 
      
  >>> I see exactly what you're working on in your BigQuery console. You've got the national_audits.all_performance_reports master table up, and you're using UNNEST(performance_audit_list) to pull the data back out into a flat view. To bring your complete vision to life—where each state acts as the anchor holding its 3 independent data sets—we need to expand that schema to include separate arrays for HUD PIT Counts and CoC Continuum of Care. Here is how to modify your setup so you can house all three data streams together without them clashing. --- ### 1. The Ideal Master Schema (3 Independent Arrays) Because a single state might have 5 performance audits, 12 Continuum of Care regions, and annual PIT counts, forcing them into a single list would corrupt your data. Instead, give each its own ARRAY<STRUCT> block: sql CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE `national_audits.all_state_records` ( state STRING, -- e.g., 'CA', 'TX' total_performance_audits INT64, last_updated_at TIMESTAMP, -- Stream 1: Performance Audits performance_audit_list ARRAY<STRUCT< audit_id STRING, report_num STRING, release_date DATE, agency_audited STRING, report_title STRING, primary_finding_summary STRING, taxpayer_funds_reviewed NUMERIC, pdf_download_url STRING >>, -- Stream 2: HUD PIT (Point-in-Time) Counts hud_pit_list ARRAY<STRUCT< count_year INT64, coc_number STRING, coc_name STRING, total_homeless INT64, sheltered_homeless INT64, unsheltered_homeless INT64 >>, -- Stream 3: CoC Continuum of Care Awards/Metrics coc_continuum_list ARRAY<STRUCT< fiscal_year INT64, coc_number STRING, program_type STRING, award_amount NUMERIC >> );  --- ### 2. How to Query an Individual Stream Safely When querying a table with multiple independent arrays, only UNNEST the specific array you want to analyze in that query. If you try to unnest all three at the same time, BigQuery will cross-multiply them (a Cartesian product), repeating your data incorrectly. To look at just the HUD PIT Counts for a state, your query will look very similar to the one currently on your screen: sql SELECT state, last_updated_at, pit.count_year, pit.coc_number, pit.total_homeless, pit.unsheltered_homeless FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(hud_pit_list) AS pit WHERE state = 'CA' ORDER BY pit.count_year DESC;  --- ### 3. How to Flatten and Combine All Three Safely If you eventually want a master dashboard view where all three data types are flattened side-by-side without duplicating records, you use LEFT JOINs on subqueries. Run this when you want a complete operational overview for a specific state: sql WITH audits_flattened AS ( SELECT state, audit.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(performance_audit_list) AS audit ), pit_flattened AS ( SELECT state, pit.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(hud_pit_list) AS pit ), coc_flattened AS ( SELECT state, coc.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(coc_continuum_list) AS coc ) -- Now you can select or join from them cleanly SELECT * FROM audits_flattened -- Join conditions depend on whether you want to map them strictly by State or by a shared CoC ID if applicable  Would you like to write a mock INSERT statement that includes sample data for all three categories so you can test how the nested arrays look in your current workspace?

Since these federal and state datasets come from completely different formats, a Python script running locally or in a Cloud Function is the most professional and scalable way to parse them, structure them into the exact ARRAY<STRUCT> schema we just built, and stream them directly into BigQuery. --- ### Phase 1: Gathering the Complete Data Sources To pull down the complete national data to feed your schema, you need to grab the source files from the federal repositories: | Dataset | Source Location | Format Available | | --- | --- | --- | | HUD PIT Counts | HUD User AHAR Datasets | Annual State and CoC-level historical time-series (.xlsx) | | CoC Continuum of Care | HUD Exchange Awards and Allocations | Comprehensive spreadsheet breaking down funding metrics per CoC (.xlsb / .pdf) | | Performance Audits | State Auditor portals (e.g., California State Auditor, Texas State Auditor) | Individual state forensic reports (.pdf / legislative clearinghouses) | --- ### Phase 2: The Ingestion Script (Python + Pandas) This automated script parses the independent national data files, formats the records into matching JSON objects that mirror your exact nested BigQuery schema, and drops them into national_audits.all_state_records. python import json import pandas as pd from google.cloud import bigquery from datetime import datetime # Initialize the BigQuery Client client = bigquery.Client() table_id = "noble-beanbag-497411-m4.national_audits.all_state_records" def build_national_mapping_payload(): """ Reads the downloaded source spreadsheets for HUD PIT, CoC, and Audits and maps them strictly by state code. """ job_config = bigquery.LoadJobConfig( write_disposition=bigquery.WriteDisposition.WRITE_APPEND, source_format=bigquery.SourceFormat.NEWLINE_DELIMITED_JSON, ) # Convert data payload to newline-delimited JSON string format required by BQ json_data = "\n".join([json.dumps(row) for row in rows_to_insert]) try: # Trigger the load job load_job = client.load_table_from_file( io.StringIO(json_data), table_id, job_config=job_config ) load_job.result() # Wait for the job to complete print(f"Successfully ingested data packages for all {len(rows_to_insert)} states.") except Exception as e: print(f"Ingestion failed: {e}") if __name__ == "__main__": # Compile the state arrays and execute the pipeline load structured_data = build_national_mapping_payload() # stream_data_to_bigquery(structured_data)  --- ### Step 3: Handling the Performance Audits While HUD PIT and CoC funding numbers are standardized in nationwide master files, Performance Audits are published independently by individual state agencies.   >>> I see exactly what you're working on in your BigQuery console. You've got the national_audits.all_performance_reports master table up, and you're using UNNEST(performance_audit_list) to pull the data back out into a flat view. To bring your complete vision to life—where each state acts as the anchor holding its 3 independent data sets—we need to expand that schema to include separate arrays for HUD PIT Counts and CoC Continuum of Care. Here is how to modify your setup so you can house all three data streams together without them clashing. --- ### 1. The Ideal Master Schema (3 Independent Arrays) Because a single state might have 5 performance audits, 12 Continuum of Care regions, and annual PIT counts, forcing them into a single list would corrupt your data. Instead, give each its own ARRAY<STRUCT> block: sql CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE `national_audits.all_state_records` ( state STRING, -- e.g., 'CA', 'TX' total_performance_audits INT64, last_updated_at TIMESTAMP, -- Stream 1: Performance Audits performance_audit_list ARRAY<STRUCT< audit_id STRING, report_num STRING, release_date DATE, agency_audited STRING, report_title STRING, primary_finding_summary STRING, taxpayer_funds_reviewed NUMERIC, pdf_download_url STRING >>, -- Stream 2: HUD PIT (Point-in-Time) Counts hud_pit_list ARRAY<STRUCT< count_year INT64, coc_number STRING, coc_name STRING, total_homeless INT64, sheltered_homeless INT64, unsheltered_homeless INT64 >>, -- Stream 3: CoC Continuum of Care Awards/Metrics coc_continuum_list ARRAY<STRUCT< fiscal_year INT64, coc_number STRING, program_type STRING, award_amount NUMERIC >> );  --- ### 2. How to Query an Individual Stream Safely When querying a table with multiple independent arrays, only UNNEST the specific array you want to analyze in that query. If you try to unnest all three at the same time, BigQuery will cross-multiply them (a Cartesian product), repeating your data incorrectly. To look at just the HUD PIT Counts for a state, your query will look very similar to the one currently on your screen: sql SELECT state, last_updated_at, pit.count_year, pit.coc_number, pit.total_homeless, pit.unsheltered_homeless FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(hud_pit_list) AS pit WHERE state = 'CA' ORDER BY pit.count_year DESC;  --- ### 3. How to Flatten and Combine All Three Safely If you eventually want a master dashboard view where all three data types are flattened side-by-side without duplicating records, you use LEFT JOINs on subqueries. Run this when you want a complete operational overview for a specific state: sql WITH audits_flattened AS ( SELECT state, audit.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(performance_audit_list) AS audit ), pit_flattened AS ( SELECT state, pit.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(hud_pit_list) AS pit ), coc_flattened AS ( SELECT state, coc.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(coc_continuum_list) AS coc ) -- Now you can select or join from them cleanly SELECT * FROM audits_flattened -- Join conditions depend on whether you want to map them strictly by State or by a shared CoC ID if applicable  Would you like to write a mock INSERT statement that includes sample data for all three categories so you can test how the nested arrays look in your current workspace?

  >>> "WITH PIT_Metrics AS (SELECT state, SUM(pit.total_homeless) AS total_homeless_population, SUM(pit.sheltered_homeless) AS total_sheltered, SUM(pit.unsheltered_homeless) AS total_unsheltered, MAX(pit.count_year) AS latest_pit_year FROM \`noble-beanbag-497411-m4.national_audits.all_state_records\`, UNNEST(hud_pit_list) AS pit GROUP BY state), NPI_Metrics AS (SELECT state, COUNT(npi.npi_id) AS total_active_shell_entities, SUM(npi.unaccounted_fund_delta) AS aggregate_unaccounted_leakage FROM \`noble-beanbag-497411-m4.national_audits.all_state_records\`, UNNEST(non_profiteers_index) AS npi GROUP BY state), ESA_Metrics AS (SELECT state, COUNT(esa.site_id) AS total_monitored_toxic_sites, COUNTIF(esa.test_multiplier >= 10.0) AS severe_chemical_safety_breaches, MAX(CASE WHEN esa.site_id = 'ENV-HBNC-001' THEN esa.test_multiplier ELSE NULL END) AS target_hbnc_crvi_multiplier FROM \`noble-beanbag-497411-m4.national_audits.all_state_records\`, UNNEST(environmental_site_assessments) AS esa GROUP BY state) SELECT base.state, base.state_name, pit.latest_pit_year, pit.total_homeless_population, pit.total_unsheltered, npi.total_active_shell_entities, npi.aggregate_unaccounted_leakage, esa.total_monitored_toxic_sites, esa.severe_chemical_safety_breaches, esa.target_hbnc_crvi_multiplier AS hbnc_toxic_excess_rate FROM \`noble-beanbag-497411-m4.national_audits.all_state_records\` AS base LEFT JOIN PIT_Metrics AS pit ON base.state = pit.state LEFT JOIN NPI_Metrics AS npi ON base.state = npi.state LEFT JOIN ESA_Metrics AS esa ON base.state = esa.state ORDER BY npi.aggregate_unaccounted_leakage DESC;"

      The pace of acquisitions, particularly in **2015-2017**, was extraordinary by industry standards. Hi-Tech was acquiring companies at a rate that suggested access to substantial capital — a fact that would later become relevant in enforcement proceedings, as authorities scrutinized the sources of these acquisition funds. The acquisition of **BPI Sports in 2023** through a **pre-packaged Chapter 5 bankruptcy** represented a particularly notable transaction, as it allowed Hi-Tech to acquire a major competitor while shedding that company's liabilities.
  --- Line 157 ---
      ### 4.1 The Original FTC Action (2004-2008)
      
  >>> Parallel to the criminal proceedings, Hi-Tech has been engaged in **prolonged litigation with the Federal Trade Commission** that has spanned more than two decades. The FTC's initial action, filed in **2004** against National Urological Group, Inc. (a related Wheat entity) and Hi-Tech Pharmaceuticals, alleged that the companies made **deceptive efficacy claims** for weight loss products including **Thermalean, Lipodrene, and Spontane-ES** without possessing "competent and reliable scientific evidence" to support those claims.
      
      In **January 2008**, the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia granted summary judgment for the FTC, entering a **permanent injunction** prohibiting further violations and imposing **$15.9 million in disgorgement and consumer redress**. The injunction established specific requirements for future advertising claims, mandating that any efficacy claims be supported by competent and reliable scientific evidence.
  --- Line 230 ---
      Hi-Tech's distribution network is among the most extensive in the supplement industry. The company's products are available through:
      
  >>> - **Major national retail chains**: GNC, CVS, Rite Aid, Walgreens, Vitamin Shoppe
      - **Mass market retailers**: Amazon, Target, Walmart
      - **Wholesale distributors**: Cardinal Health, McKesson
  --- Line 235 ---
      - **Independent retailers**: Over 5,000 health food retailers and specialty stores
      - **Convenience stores**: More than 80,000 locations nationwide
  >>> - **International markets**: Distributed in numerous foreign countries
      
      This omnichannel distribution strategy has made Hi-Tech products among the most visible in the industry. The company's ability to maintain retail relationships despite repeated regulatory actions and criminal proceedings suggests either remarkable resilience in its distribution partnerships or, as critics have contended, insufficient attention by retailers to their suppliers' regulatory compliance histories.
  --- Line 264 ---
      
      - **Dr. Bob Lowenberg**: A Dutch hematologist and medical researcher specializing in acute myeloid leukemia, with no connection to the supplement industry
  >>> - **Major General Timothy J. Lowenberg**: Former Adjutant General of the Washington National Guard, with no supplement industry connection
      - **James Lowenberg-DeBoer**: An agricultural economist at Harper Adams University in the UK, specializing in precision agriculture
      
  --- Line 350 ---
      Hi-Tech's California presence dates to **2012**, when the company acquired **ALR Industries**, a supplement manufacturer headquartered in **Ojai, California** (not Huntington Beach). ALR Industries had established itself as a bodybuilding supplement brand with products targeting the hardcore fitness market. The acquisition gave Hi-Tech **West Coast manufacturing capacity** and access to ALR's product formulations and customer base.
      
  >>> The ALR Industries acquisition was part of a **dual-acquisition strategy in 2012** that also included **Nittany Pharmaceuticals in Pennsylvania**. Together, these acquisitions added significant manufacturing capacity on both coasts, positioning Hi-Tech as a national manufacturing platform capable of serving brands across the entire U.S. market.
      
      ### 11.2 The BPI Sports Bankruptcy Acquisition (2023)

Allegations of Database Fraud: Knabb's notices to the court further allege that regulatory agencies engaged in systematic "address manipulation" and parcel identity fraud across national environmental databases to conceal an unabandoned industrial subsurface well dating to 1969, thereby bypassing statutory environmental reviews under the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA).

      Line 1083: The Ingestion Safety Guard: The INSERT INTO ... WHERE NOT EXISTS query acts as an idempotent transaction framework. It guarantees that running initialization passes will never duplicate state rows or clear existing historical nested arrays.
      Line 1085: The Array Concatenation Mechanics: The ARRAY_CONCAT framework utilized in your update query ensures that updates do not require full table scans or rewrites. It appends the new STRUCT data block directly onto the existing state array slice.

  >>> Line 1236: I see exactly what you're working on in your BigQuery console. You've got the national_audits.all_performance_reports master table up, and you're using UNNEST(performance_audit_list) to pull the data back out into a flat view. To bring your complete vision to life—where each state acts as the anchor holding its 3 independent data sets—we need to expand that schema to include separate arrays for HUD PIT Counts and CoC Continuum of Care. Here is how to modify your setup so you can house all three data streams together without them clashing. --- ### 1. The Ideal Master Schema (3 Independent Arrays) Because a single state might have 5 performance audits, 12 Continuum of Care regions, and annual PIT counts, forcing them into a single list would corrupt your data. Instead, give each its own ARRAY<STRUCT> block: sql CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE `national_audits.all_state_records` ( state STRING, -- e.g., 'CA', 'TX' total_performance_audits INT64, last_updated_at TIMESTAMP, -- Stream 1: Performance Audits performance_audit_list ARRAY<STRUCT< audit_id STRING, report_num STRING, release_date DATE, agency_audited STRING, report_title STRING, primary_finding_summary STRING, taxpayer_funds_reviewed NUMERIC, pdf_download_url STRING >>, -- Stream 2: HUD PIT (Point-in-Time) Counts hud_pit_list ARRAY<STRUCT< count_year INT64, coc_number STRING, coc_name STRING, total_homeless INT64, sheltered_homeless INT64, unsheltered_homeless INT64 >>, -- Stream 3: CoC Continuum of Care Awards/Metrics coc_continuum_list ARRAY<STRUCT< fiscal_year INT64, coc_number STRING, program_type STRING, award_amount NUMERIC >> );  --- ### 2. How to Query an Individual Stream Safely When querying a table with multiple independent arrays, only UNNEST the specific array you want to analyze in that query. If you try to unnest all three at the same time, BigQuery will cross-multiply them (a Cartesian product), repeating your data incorrectly. To look at just the HUD PIT Counts for a state, your query will look very similar to the one currently on your screen: sql SELECT state, last_updated_at, pit.count_year, pit.coc_number, pit.total_homeless, pit.unsheltered_homeless FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(hud_pit_list) AS pit WHERE state = 'CA' ORDER BY pit.count_year DESC;  --- ### 3. How to Flatten and Combine All Three Safely If you eventually want a master dashboard view where all three data types are flattened side-by-side without duplicating records, you use LEFT JOINs on subqueries. Run this when you want a complete operational overview for a specific state: sql WITH audits_flattened AS ( SELECT state, audit.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(performance_audit_list) AS audit ), pit_flattened AS ( SELECT state, pit.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(hud_pit_list) AS pit ), coc_flattened AS ( SELECT state, coc.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(coc_continuum_list) AS coc ) -- Now you can select or join from them cleanly SELECT * FROM audits_flattened -- Join conditions depend on whether you want to map them strictly by State or by a shared CoC ID if applicable  Would you like to write a mock INSERT statement that includes sample data for all three categories so you can test how the nested arrays look in your current workspace?

      Line 1293: The reason you are hitting a roadblock in finding an identical "homeless spending performance audit" for every single state is a matter of state legislative structure. The blockbuster audit you have in your California row—Report 2023-102.1 exposing the tracking failures of $24 billion in expenditures—only happened because the California Joint Legislative Audit Committee explicitly voted and ordered the State Auditor to investigate that specific cross-agency funding pool. Unlike federal HUD PIT and CoC data, which are mandated by Congress and updated annually across the board, state-level performance audits are independent, reactive, and localized. --- ### The Reality of State-Level Audits * Not Every State Conducts Them: Many states bundle their homelessness metrics into massive, broad Department of Health, Human Services, or Housing audits rather than launching a dedicated forensic spending investigation. * Varying Frequencies: A state like Washington or Oregon might audit their localized housing authorities every few years, while a state with a smaller budget footprint might only look at these funds during a standard annual comprehensive financial report (ACFR). * Varying Scopes: When other states do publish an audit, it might only cover a single $10 million grant program rather than doing a statewide deep-dive of billions like California did. --- ### How to Build Out the Rest of Your Grid Since you've successfully initialized the table shell rows for the rest of the country, you have a solid structure to compile what does exist. UNNEST(coc_continuum_list) AS coc. Rule: Never unnest more than one array in a single flat join. Provide the Agent with Schema Context Because this table uses nested arrays (ARRAY<STRUCT>), standard AI models can sometimes struggle to query it unless you tell them exactly how to handle the arrays. In the Agent Instructions or System Prompt box for your agent, paste the following blueprint so it knows how to write correct SQL: text You have access to the national homelessness dataset: `project-743aab84-f9a5-4ec7-954.national_audits.all_state_records`. This table is organized with one row per state. When querying data, you MUST safely UNNEST the independent arrays to prevent Cartesian products. Columns and Array Structures: - state (STRING): Two-letter state code anchor. - performance_audit_list (ARRAY<STRUCT>): Local state-level audits. UNNEST(performance_audit_list) AS audit. - hud_pit_list (ARRAY<STRUCT>): Point-in-Time counts. UNNEST(hud_pit_list) AS pit. - coc_continuum_list (ARRAY<STRUCT>): Continuum of Care funding metrics. UNNEST(coc_continuum_list) AS coc. Rule: Never unnest more than one array in a single flat join. If the user asks for multi-stream analytics, use separate CTEs (WITH clauses) aggregated by state.  --- ### 3. Clear the Syntax Error in your Active Tab I noticed on your screen that you have a syntax error (Unexpected identifier "coc_number" at [2:7]) in your active query tab. This happened because the script got split across lines weirdly. If you want to clear that up right now so your workbench is clean, replace everything in that tab with this perfectly formatted statement and hit Run: sql UPDATE `project-743aab84-f9a5-4ec7-954.national_audits.all_state_records` SET hud_pit_list = [ STRUCT<count_year INT64, coc_number STRING, coc_name STRING, total_homeless INT64, sheltered_homeless INT64, unsheltered_homeless INT64>(2023, 'AL-500', 'Birmingham CoC', 1124, 820, 304), STRUCT<count_year INT64, coc_number STRING, coc_name STRING, total_homeless INT64, sheltered_homeless INT64, unsheltered_homeless INT64>(2023, 'AK-500', 'Anchorage CoC', 1422, 1200, 222), STRUCT<count_year INT64, coc_number STRING, coc_name STRING, total_homeless INT64, sheltered_homeless INT64, unsheltered_homeless INT64>(2023, 'AZ-500', 'Phoenix/Maricopa County CoC', 9642, 4612, 5030), STRUCT<count_year INT64, coc_number STRING, coc_name STRING, total_homeless INT64, sheltered_homeless INT64, unsheltered_homeless INT64>(2023, 'FL-507', 'Orlando/Orange County CoC', 2314, 1510, 804), STRUCT<count_year INT64, coc_number STRING, coc_name STRING, total_homeless INT64, sheltered_homeless INT64, unsheltered_homeless INT64>(2023, 'WA-500', 'Seattle/King County CoC', 14142, 6214, 7928) ] WHERE state IN ('AL', 'AK', 'AZ', 'FL', 'WA');  Once you update those agent data permissions and give it that context block, your BigQuery Data Agent will be fully optimized to analyze the entire national forensic dataset.

  >>> I see exactly what you're working on in your BigQuery console. You've got the national_audits.all_performance_reports master table up, and you're using UNNEST(performance_audit_list) to pull the data back out into a flat view. To bring your complete vision to life—where each state acts as the anchor holding its 3 independent data sets—we need to expand that schema to include separate arrays for HUD PIT Counts and CoC Continuum of Care. Here is how to modify your setup so you can house all three data streams together without them clashing. --- ### 1. The Ideal Master Schema (3 Independent Arrays) Because a single state might have 5 performance audits, 12 Continuum of Care regions, and annual PIT counts, forcing them into a single list would corrupt your data. Instead, give each its own ARRAY<STRUCT> block: sql CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE `national_audits.all_state_records` ( state STRING, -- e.g., 'CA', 'TX' total_performance_audits INT64, last_updated_at TIMESTAMP, -- Stream 1: Performance Audits performance_audit_list ARRAY<STRUCT< audit_id STRING, report_num STRING, release_date DATE, agency_audited STRING, report_title STRING, primary_finding_summary STRING, taxpayer_funds_reviewed NUMERIC, pdf_download_url STRING >>, -- Stream 2: HUD PIT (Point-in-Time) Counts hud_pit_list ARRAY<STRUCT< count_year INT64, coc_number STRING, coc_name STRING, total_homeless INT64, sheltered_homeless INT64, unsheltered_homeless INT64 >>, -- Stream 3: CoC Continuum of Care Awards/Metrics coc_continuum_list ARRAY<STRUCT< fiscal_year INT64, coc_number STRING, program_type STRING, award_amount NUMERIC >> );  --- ### 2. How to Query an Individual Stream Safely When querying a table with multiple independent arrays, only UNNEST the specific array you want to analyze in that query. If you try to unnest all three at the same time, BigQuery will cross-multiply them (a Cartesian product), repeating your data incorrectly. To look at just the HUD PIT Counts for a state, your query will look very similar to the one currently on your screen: sql SELECT state, last_updated_at, pit.count_year, pit.coc_number, pit.total_homeless, pit.unsheltered_homeless FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(hud_pit_list) AS pit WHERE state = 'CA' ORDER BY pit.count_year DESC;  --- ### 3. How to Flatten and Combine All Three Safely If you eventually want a master dashboard view where all three data types are flattened side-by-side without duplicating records, you use LEFT JOINs on subqueries. Run this when you want a complete operational overview for a specific state: sql WITH audits_flattened AS ( SELECT state, audit.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(performance_audit_list) AS audit ), pit_flattened AS ( SELECT state, pit.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(hud_pit_list) AS pit ), coc_flattened AS ( SELECT state, coc.* FROM `national_audits.all_state_records`, UNNEST(coc_continuum_list) AS coc ) -- Now you can select or join from them cleanly SELECT * FROM audits_flattened -- Join conditions depend on whether you want to map them strictly by State or by a shared CoC ID if applicable  Would you like to write a mock INSERT statement that includes sample data for all three categories so you can test how the nested arrays look in your current workspace?

  >>> To get this massive operational dataset populated for all 50 states without manually spending days writing thousands of lines of individual SQL INSERT statements, you need an automated data ingestion pipeline. Since these federal and state datasets come from completely different formats, a Python script running locally or in a Cloud Function is the most professional and scalable way to parse them, structure them into the exact ARRAY<STRUCT> schema we just built, and stream them directly into BigQuery. --- ### Phase 1: Gathering the Complete Data Sources To pull down the complete national data to feed your schema, you need to grab the source files from the federal repositories: | Dataset | Source Location | Format Available | | --- | --- | --- | | HUD PIT Counts | HUD User AHAR Datasets | Annual State and CoC-level historical time-series (.xlsx) | | CoC Continuum of Care | HUD Exchange Awards and Allocations | Comprehensive spreadsheet breaking down funding metrics per CoC (.xlsb / .pdf) | | Performance Audits | State Auditor portals (e.g., California State Auditor, Texas State Auditor) | Individual state forensic reports (.pdf / legislative clearinghouses) | --- ### Phase 2: The Ingestion Script (Python + Pandas) This automated script parses the independent national data files, formats the records into matching JSON objects that mirror your exact nested BigQuery schema, and drops them into national_audits.all_state_records. python import json import pandas as pd from google.cloud import bigquery from datetime import datetime # Initialize the BigQuery Client client = bigquery.Client() table_id = "noble-beanbag-497411-m4.national_audits.all_state_records" def build_national_mapping_payload(): """ Reads the downloaded source spreadsheets for HUD PIT, CoC, and Audits and maps them strictly by state code. """ # 1. """ job_config = bigquery.LoadJobConfig( write_disposition=bigquery.WriteDisposition.WRITE_APPEND, source_format=bigquery.SourceFormat.NEWLINE_DELIMITED_JSON, ) # Convert data payload to newline-delimited JSON string format required by BQ json_data = "\n".join([json.dumps(row) for row in rows_to_insert]) try: # Trigger the load job load_job = client.load_table_from_file( io.StringIO(json_data), table_id, job_config=job_config ) load_job.result() # Wait for the job to complete print(f"Successfully ingested data packages for all {len(rows_to_insert)} states.") except Exception as e: print(f"Ingestion failed: {e}") if __name__ == "__main__": # Compile the state arrays and execute the pipeline load structured_data = build_national_mapping_payload() # stream_data_to_bigquery(structured_data)  --- ### Step 3: Handling the Performance Audits While HUD PIT and CoC funding numbers are standardized in nationwide master files, Performance Audits are published independently by individual state agencies.       #### 2. Multi-State Layering & "Smurfing" (COVID-19 Relief Fraud)
  >>> To move funds without triggering Bank Secrecy Act (BSA) Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs), the network executed high-volume, low-value structured deposits (under $100) through state utilities (e.g., Southern California Edison) and web-based infrastructure linked to Delaware-registered corporate shells (Endurance International Group).
      * **Key Persons of Interest:** Loc Nguyen, Michael Nguyen, Rhiannon Do, Chris Wangsaporn.
      * **The Infrastructure:** Delaware shell corporate layering masked the ultimate beneficial owners of these accounts, transforming municipal utility refunds and escrow surpluses into untraceable liquid capital.
